Common Workflows

Practical examples of how to use Ralph for different development tasks

01

Adding a New Feature

The most common use case for Ralph

When adding new functionality, Ralph excels at implementing features from scratch based on your specifications. Here's the recommended workflow:

Step 1

Create a Feature Branch

bash 
git checkout -b feature/user-notifications
Step 2

Write Your PROMPT.md

markdown PROMPT.md 
# Add User Notification System

## Goal
Implement a notification system that alerts users about important events.

## Requirements
- Create a notifications table in the database
- Add API endpoints: GET /notifications, POST /notifications/mark-read
- Display notification count in the header
- Show notification dropdown with recent items
- Mark notifications as read when clicked

## Technical Details
- Use existing Prisma schema patterns
- Follow the UserController pattern for NotificationController
- Store in PostgreSQL with created_at timestamp

## Constraints
- No email notifications yet (future task)
- No real-time updates (polling is fine for now)
- Max 50 notifications shown in dropdown

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Notifications appear in the header
- [ ] Clicking marks as read
- [ ] Count badge updates correctly
Step 3

Run Ralph

bash 
# Use Standard mode for typical features
ralph -S
Step 4

Review and Test

bash 
# See what Ralph did
git diff HEAD~1

# Run your tests
npm test

# Try it manually
npm run dev
Step 5

Merge When Ready

bash 
git checkout main
git merge feature/user-notifications
git push

Tips for Feature Development

  • Break large features into smaller specs
  • Reference existing patterns in your codebase
  • Include acceptance criteria Ralph can verify
02

Fixing a Bug

Quick iterations to squash issues

For bug fixes, you want Ralph to understand the current (broken) behavior and the expected behavior. Be specific about reproduction steps.

markdown PROMPT.md 
# Fix: Login form doesn't show validation errors

## Problem
When a user submits the login form with invalid credentials,
no error message is shown. The form just clears and nothing happens.

## Current Behavior
1. User enters wrong password
2. Clicks "Login"
3. Form clears, no feedback shown
4. User doesn't know what went wrong

## Expected Behavior
1. User enters wrong password
2. Clicks "Login"
3. Error message appears: "Invalid email or password"
4. Form keeps the email field populated

## Location
- src/components/LoginForm.tsx
- src/api/auth.ts (error handling)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Error message displays on invalid credentials
- [ ] Email field is preserved after error
- [ ] Success still redirects to dashboard

For quick bug fixes, use Quick mode:

ralph -Q

Tips for Bug Fixes

  • Include exact reproduction steps
  • Specify which files are likely involved
  • Describe both current and expected behavior
  • Quick mode (-Q) is usually enough for bugs
03

Refactoring Code

Improve structure without changing behavior

Refactoring with Ralph requires clear boundaries. You're changing how code works, not what it does. Be explicit about preserving behavior.

markdown PROMPT.md 
# Refactor: Extract payment processing into service

## Goal
Move payment logic from OrderController into a dedicated PaymentService
for better separation of concerns and testability.

## Current State
- Payment logic is in src/controllers/OrderController.ts (lines 150-280)
- Stripe API calls are inline with order logic
- Hard to test payment logic in isolation

## Desired State
- New file: src/services/PaymentService.ts
- PaymentService handles all Stripe interactions
- OrderController calls PaymentService methods
- Existing tests still pass

## What to Move
- processPayment() function
- refundPayment() function
- validatePaymentMethod() function
- Stripe API initialization

## Constraints
- DO NOT change any public API endpoints
- DO NOT modify database schema
- All existing tests must pass
- Keep the same error handling behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New PaymentService file exists
- [ ] OrderController uses PaymentService
- [ ] All existing order tests pass
- [ ] No change to API responses

Use Thorough mode for refactoring to ensure nothing breaks:

ralph -T

Tips for Refactoring

  • Be very explicit about what should NOT change
  • Reference existing tests that must pass
  • Use Thorough mode (-T) for more validation
  • Review changes carefully before merging
04

Writing Tests

Let Ralph write your test cases

Ralph can analyze your code and write comprehensive tests. Just point it at the code and describe what kind of coverage you want.

markdown PROMPT.md 
# Add unit tests for UserService

## Goal
Write comprehensive unit tests for src/services/UserService.ts

## Target File
src/services/UserService.ts

## Test File Location
src/services/__tests__/UserService.test.ts

## Methods to Test
- createUser()
- updateUser()
- deleteUser()
- findUserByEmail()
- validatePassword()

## Test Coverage Requirements
- Happy path for each method
- Error cases (invalid input, not found, etc.)
- Edge cases (empty strings, special characters)
- Mock external dependencies (database, email service)

## Constraints
- Use Jest and existing test patterns
- Mock Prisma client, don't hit real database
- Follow AAA pattern (Arrange, Act, Assert)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All methods have at least 2 tests each
- [ ] Error handling is tested
- [ ] Mocks are properly set up
- [ ] Tests pass: npm test

Tips for Test Writing

  • Specify your testing framework (Jest, Mocha, etc.)
  • Mention existing test patterns in your codebase
  • List specific scenarios you want covered
  • Include edge cases explicitly
05

Quick Reference

Which mode to use for each task type

Task Type Recommended Mode Why
Quick bug fix -Q (Quick) Simple changes, fast iteration
New feature -S (Standard) Balanced quality and speed
Refactoring -T (Thorough) Extra validation to avoid breaking changes
Complex architecture -L (Long) Maximum iterations for difficult problems
Writing tests -S (Standard) Enough cycles to cover edge cases
Overnight run -T (Thorough) Quality matters more than speed

Ready to streamline your workflow?

Pick a task, write a spec, and let Ralph handle the implementation.

Write Your Spec → Get Started

Ralph Workflow

Multi-agent AI orchestration for automated code development. Ship your ideas with autonomous agents that collaborate until quality is achieved.
